beats.text: You just wrote the same fact two ways with three different rectangles. Now let's go back to the rectangle you started with, the 4-by-5, and say the One Thing one more time. The equal sign means both sides have the same value. That's true for every fact you write today and every fact you'll write tomorrow.

params.description: Paired equation display appears showing 4 × 5 = 20 and 20 = 4 × 5 side by side.

options: the same fact written two ways
a wrong one and a right one
params.description: Dropdown fill-in-the-blank appears with stem and three options.

condition.selected: the same fact written two ways

validator.description: Student selected 'the same fact written two ways', correct

beats.text: Right. The same fact written two ways. You can write it both ways. The equal sign means both sides have the same value, and that's true no matter which way you write it.

elements.description: Dropdown showing stem 'These two equations are ___' with student's selection confirmed.
options: the same fact written two ways
a wrong one and a right one

beats.text: You can now write the same fact two ways. That's a skill you'll use in every lesson that comes next. Tomorrow, you'll start finding ALL the rectangles that share the same area, not just one. And every time you check a rectangle, you'll see both ways the equation can be written, just like today. The equal sign means both sides have the same value. Same fact, both ways around. You're ready.
